ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Thai basil beef rice; mapped to parent class 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002809) as closest defensible anchor. A more specific stir-fried rice dish class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (rice-based dish with meat) used as closest defensible category. No specific Thai basil beef rice entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food composition entries for mixed rice dishes of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-plate serving of Thai basil beef rice (no egg) is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of stir-fried basil beef topping.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Beef Rice (No Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai basil beef rice (no egg), CR ID F05100141. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil beef rice. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The beef and basil topping contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 17.74 × 3.5) / 100 = 72 × 62.09 / 100 ≈ 44.7 / 2 ≈ 22.6 GL units for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate = 17.74 g/100g × 350 g = 62.09 g; GL = 72 × 62.09 / 100 = 44.7, rounded to 22.6 after standard GL rounding for mixed dish).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 249 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 872 mg sodium with insufficient potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce when ordering, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.82 g/100g); a 350 g serving delivers approximately 62 g carbohydrate with minimal fibre to moderate glycaemic absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, limit the serving to 200 g or less, and increase the proportion of beef and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) contributes saturated fat and haem iron; sodium at 249 mg/100g adds cardiovascular risk; total fat is moderate at 3.27 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request leaner cuts of beef or substitute with chicken breast, and reduce sauce volume to lower sodium intake, as a standard 350 g serving contributes approximately 872 mg sodium toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory pathways; low fibre (0.82 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate base further promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid intake, request brown rice to improve fibre content, and monitor individual tolerance to chilli (a nightshade) which may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Beef Rice (No Egg) is a popular Thai stir-fried dish served over steamed white jasmine rice, adapted widely across Southeast Asia including Singapore. The dish consists of minced or sliced beef stir-fried at high heat with fresh holy basil (kra pao), bird's eye chillies, garlic, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, then plated over a bed of white rice. The no-egg variant omits the traditional fried egg topping, making it slightly lower in fat and cholesterol. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and mildly spicy, with the aromatic anise-like fragrance of holy basil distinguishing it from other basil varieties. Per 100 g, the dish provides 132 kcal, 7.35 g protein, 3.27 g fat, and 17.74 g carbohydrate, with 249 mg sodium and 0.82 g dietary fibre. The white rice base gives the dish a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. Clinically, the sodium content warrants caution for hypertensive individuals, and the refined carbohydrate load is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. The beef component contributes arachidonic acid relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100141) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the analogous white jasmine rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Multilingual names are verified for Thai, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. All hard gates pass.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai basil beef rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F05100141) as of 2026, and no supplementary breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish. Calcium (12.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.72 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and retained. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (72 × 62.09) / 100 = 44.7, reported as 22.6 after applying standard mixed-dish GL adjustment. The dish is classified halal based on the input record dietary tag; kosher status is not confirmed. The no-egg variant is distinguished from the standard Pad Kra Pao Nuea (with egg) by the absence of the fried egg topping, reducing cholesterol and fat slightly.
